content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Self-Querying Prompts for Improved Uncertainty Calibration in LLMs",
  "problem": "Language models often generate overconfident outputs and lack reliable uncertainty estimates, especially on ambiguous or out-of-domain (OOD) inputs. Current prompting methods do not directly elicit calibrated confidence from LLMs.",
  "motivation": "Accurate uncertainty estimation is essential for trustworthy AI, impacting decision-making in high-stakes tasks. LLMs are increasingly deployed in such settings, yet their probabilistic self-estimates remain poorly calibrated. Prompt-based approaches offer a lightweight alternative to retraining or ensembling for uncertainty quantification.",
  "method": "We propose a counterfactual self-querying prompt: for each input, append a request for the model to generate its answer, then also rate its confidence (e.g., 'On a 0-100% scale, how sure are you?'), and finally generate a plausible but incorrect alternative. The model's ability to generate convincing alternatives alongside a confidence score will be used to calibrate its uncertainty: high confidence should only be permitted if alternative completions are difficult to generate. We compare this to standard confidence elicitation and self-consistency prompting.",
  "experiment_plan": "Test on a small set of ambiguous and OOD samples from datasets like BoolQ and TriviaQA. Compare the calibration error (e.g., Expected Calibration Error) and discrimination (AUC-ROC for correctness vs. confidence) of the proposed prompt versus standard confidence queries and self-consistency ensembling. Evaluate whether the ability to generate convincing counterfactuals correlates with lower ground truth accuracy and improved calibration."
}
